Facts.text: Hugh Capet, is coronated as King of France, in Paris, after being elected by the nobility
Battle at Hagenoorde: German emperor beats earl Egbert II
EmperorFerdinand IIopens German Parliament
Sweden and Denmark sign peace treaty
Norway's oldest newspaper still in print, Adresseavisen, is founded (first edition published this date)
Pitcairn Island is discovered by Midshipman Robert Pitcairn on an expeditionary voyage in the Pacific, commanded by Philip Carteret
George Washington takes command of Continental Army at Cambridge, Massachusetts
British forces massacre 360 men, women and children in Wyoming, Pennsylvania
Prussia declares war on Austria
Michael Keens exhibits the first large-scale cultivated strawberry, a large fruit strawberry called the Keen Seedling
Americans capture Fort Erie, Canada (War of 1812)
First savings bank in NY, the Bank for Savings in the City of New-York, opens its doors
First state normal school in the US opens in Lexington, Massachusetts, with three students
John Couch Adams decides to determine position of an unknown planet by irregularities it causes in the motion of Uranus
The last pair of Great Auks is killed
Slaves freed in Danish West Indies (now US Virgin Islands)
French forces enter Rome to restore Pope Pius IX to power, providing a major obstacle to Italian unification
Congress authorizes US's 2nd mint (San Francisco, California)
Colonel Jackson receives his commission as brigadier general
Martinsburg, Virginia - Confederate forces pull out before US advance
Battle of Chattahoochie River, Georgia [until Jul 9]
Harpers Ferry, West Virginia - Federals evacuate in face of Early's advance
Montenegro declares war on Turkey
SS Daphne sinks on Clyde River in Scotland; 195 die
Dow Jones publishes its first stock index, the Dow Jones Transportation Average
First New-York Tribune printed using the first commercial Linotype machine
Idaho admitted as 43rd US state
American troops, en route to the Philippines on the SS China, raise the American flag on Wake Island
Dutch Automobile Club forms (KNAC)
US Navy defeats Spanish fleet in Santiago harbor, Cuba
The British evacuate Rustenburg and occupy Commando Nek and Silkaatsnek in the Anglo-Boer war
Trying to stem the growing popular resentment, Tsar Nicholas of Russia issues a decree that abolishes the banishment of dissidents and troublemakers to Siberia
American boxer Marvin Hart scores a 12th round KO of Jack Root in Reno, Nevada for the vacant world heavyweight title
Kuyper government forms in Holland
Papal decree forbids the modernization of theology
Indian nationalist Bal Gangadhar Tilak arrested for sedition by the British in wake of Muzzafarpur bombing
A Common Tern is banded in Maine and later found dead in Africa in 1919, making it the first bird known to have crossed the Atlantic

Confederate veterans at the Great Reunion of 1913 reenact Pickett's Charge; upon reaching the high-water mark of the Confederacy they are met by the outstretched hands of friendship from Union survivors

After exploding a bomb in US Senate reception room previous day, Erich Muenter, a German instructor at Cornell University, shoots JP Morgan for representing the British government in war contract negotiations
Spontaneous demonstration at Tauride Palace, Petrograd
SDAP'er Suze Groenweg elected 1st woman in Dutch parliament
Java Technical School, Bandung, opens
Royal Air Force holds an air display at Hendon, England
Dockers' strike in Hull, Grimsby, Cardiff and Bristol over to London
Dunlop Latex Development Laboratories make foam rubber
US Veterans Administration created
After 30 years as manager of the New York Giants, John McGraw retires from baseball (2,583 wins / 1,948 losses NY)
FDIC pays off 1st insured depositors, Fon du Lac Bank, East Peoria, Illinois
British East Coast Main Line locomotive No. 4468 "Mallard" reached a speed of 202.6 km/h (125.9 mph) setting new record for steam powered trains
ARP-leader and Former Dutch PM Hendrikus Colijn argues cooperation with Germany
German occupiers forbid using Dutch royal names
Adolf Hitlervisits Field Marshal Von Bock's headquarters in Ukraine
German troops march into Sebastopol, Crimea
British Liberator bomber sinks German U-628 in Bay of Biscay
Oriole Park, then a minor league baseball stadium burns down in Baltimore; cause, speculated discarded cigarette
US V-III-E Army corp opens assault on Coutances Cotentin
First Dutch government of Louis Beel forms
The Cape Passive Resistance Council is formed at a mass meeting in Cape Town, attended by 1,500 people
252,288 people, a record number, pass through Grand Central Terminal train station in NYC
Soviet Union doesn't participate in Marshall Plan
Kidnapper Caryl Chessman sentenced to death, California; execution doesn't happen until 1960
American ocean liner SS United States departs New York Harbor on her maiden crossing of Atlantic Ocean; completes voyage in record time of 82 hours 40 minutes
Puerto Rico's constitution approved by US Congress
First exhibition of Karen, the bongo antelope, opens at Cleveland Zoo in Ohio
Algerian Revolution against French rule ends (Algeria gains independence on 5th July)
National Airways Corporation Dakota DC-3 crashes in the Kaimai Ranges in New Zealand, killing all 23 passengers and crew in New Zealand’s worst domestic civil aviation accident

Harold Strachan, member of the Communist Party of South Africa and Umkhonto we Sizwe, the armed wing of the African National Congress, is served with a restriction order in terms of the Suppression of Communism Act
USSR and Persia sign dam building and economic aid pact
Atlanta Braves Tony Cloninger, 1st NL player and only pitcher to hit 2 grand slams in a MLB game (17-3 v SF Giants)
Race riots in Omaha, Nebraska
"News at 10" premieres on British TV
41°F lowest temperature ever recorded in Cleveland in July

As part of a series of protests against housing conditions in Derry, the Derry Housing Action Committee (DHAC) hold a sit-down protest on the newly opened second deck of the Craigavon Bridge in the city, Northern Ireland
Cleveland Indians pitcher Luis Tiant strikes out MLB record 19 Minnesota Twins in 1-0 win; record for 10 inning game; also record 32 Ks in consecutive games
Soviet N1 rocket explodes just after launch off at Baikonur Cosmodrome - one of the largest-ever non-nuclear human-made explosion, debris spread over 10 km
British aircraft crashes at Barcelona, 112 killed
California Angels pitcher Clyde Wright no-hits Oakland A's, 4-0
The British Army impose curfew on Falls Road area of Belfast as they search for weapons; coming under attack from the Official IRA (OIRA) and republican rioters
The Ulster Defence Association and the British Army come into conflict about a 'no-go' area at Ainsworth Avenue, Belfast
Themba Sono is forced to resign as president of South African Student Organisation (SASO)
Brothers Gaylord (Hall of Fame) and Jim Perry (3-time All Star) face each other for only time in MLB, Tigers beat Indians 5-4, Gaylord charged with loss
General Walters, serves as acting director of CIA
Soviet spacecraft Soyuz 14 carries two cosmonauts to space station Salyut 3
Adolfo Suárez becomes premier of Spain

Israel launches hostage rescue mission of 106 Air France crew and passengers held at Entebbe Airport in Uganda by pro-Palestinian hijackers. Three hostages die along with all the hijackers, numerous Ugandan soldiers and Yonatan Netanyahu, an Israeli soldier
The Senegalese Republican Movement (MRS) is founded
US Supreme Court rules 5-4, FCC had a right to reprimand NY radio station WBAI for broadcasting George Carlin's "Filthy Words" in 1973
Biggest MLB crowd in 7 years, 73,096 watch Indians beat NY Yankees, 7-0 at Cleveland Stadium
NYC transit fare rises from 60 cents to 75 cents, new brass Y-cut-out token
Riot at building site of Stopera concert hall in Amsterdam causes Ÿ1 million in damages
Texas Rangers explode for MLB single extra innings record 12 runs in 15th inning of 16-4 win v Oakland
After 22 years, England Test cricket spinner Derek Underwood hits a maiden 1st class century (111) for Kent v Sussex at Hastings
Dolphin rocket launched off San Clemente Island
Supreme Court rules Jaycees may be forced to admit women as members
Tinker Bell's first nightly flight at Walt Disney World Resort, Florida
Kuwait's National Assembly (Majlis al Umma) dissolves
Two men become the first hot-air balloon travelers to cross the Atlantic
Gene Nelson of the Oakland A's becomes first AL pitcher to steal a base since 1973 in 9-8 win over Toronto Blue Jays
US Vincennes in Strait of Hormoez shoots Iran Airbus A300, kills 290
Movie "Batman" sets record for quickest $100 million (10 days)
Peter Koech of Kenya sets 3,000m steeplechase world record of 8:05.39 in Stockholm, Sweden
US Supreme Court rules states do not have to provide funds for abortions
Liberian President Samuel Kanyon Doe offers to resign in response to rebel incursions in his country; he is later kidnapped and executed
Members of 2 Live Crew are formally charged with obscenity in Florida
Gateway Board of Trustees approve a 20-year lease for the Cleveland Indians to play MLB at Jacobs Field
South Africa's FIFA membership reinstated (expelled 1961); united non-racial FA, political prisoners released, free political parties
FIFA World Cup: In a huge upset Romania eliminates Argentina 3-2 from the round of 16 at the Rose Bowl, Pasadena, California
The deadliest day in Texas traffic history, according to the Texas Department of Public Safety. Forty six people killed in crashes.

UK House of Commons announces that the Stone of Scone, aka the Stone of Destiny, used in the coronation of Scottish and subsequently English and British monarchs, will be returned to Scotland after 700 years in Westminster Abbey
Mississippi becomes 1st state to settle tobacco suit
"Svoboda", oldest Ukrainian language newspaper in US, founded in 1893 in Jersey City, New Jersey, contracts from daily to weekly publication
A Vladivostok Avia Tupolev TU-154 jetliner crashes on approach to landing at Irkutsk, Russia killing 145 people

At a meeting of its oil ministers, OPEC agrees to maintain current production quotas; ministers indicate that, if Iraqi oil returns to the market, they may cut production in response to maintain their desired level of prices
Official opening of Bangkok's subway system
The national law legalizing same-sex marriage takes effect in Spain
Asteroid labeled 2004 XP14 flies 432,308 km (268,624 miles) past Earth
"Just Got Started Lovin' You" single released by James Otto (Billboard Song of the Year 2008)
Mark II.5 SkyTrain cars enter service in Metro Vancouver
Antonio Esfandiari wins a record $18.3 million after winning the 2012 World Series of Poker $1,000,000 Buy-In
Truck bombing kills 25 people and wounds 40 in Diwaniyah, Iraq
Germany passes first nation-wide minimum wage law – at 8.5 euros
Adelaide Crows Australian Rules Football coach Phil Walsh is murdered by his son Cy at his Somerton Park home
Bus crash in traffic jam and resulting fire kills 18 near Muenchberg, Germany
Australian cricket batsman Aaron Finch breaks his own T20 International record smashing 172 from 76 balls vs Zimbabwe in Harare, opening stand with D'Arcy Short (223) also a record
French PresidentEmmanuel Macronvisits the New Afrika Shrine nightclub in Lagos during his two-day tour of Nigeria
Souad Abderrahim is elected the first female mayor of Tunis, Tunisia
Worst monsoon rains in decades swamp Lahore, Pakistan, killing 6
Atlético Madrid sign Portugal forward Joao Felix for €126m (£113m) from Benfica in the fifth most expensive transfer in football history
Heavy monsoon rains strike Mumbai and surrounding Maharashtra state in India killing at least 43, worst flooding in a decade
Italian David-Maria Sassoli chosen as new European Parliament President
More than a million people ordered to evacuate Japanese island of Kyushu amid warnings of mudslides after 1,000mm (39in) of rainfall
US produces the most waste per head globally and recycles the least at 35% according to new research by Verisk Maplecroft, Germany recycles the most at 68%
Major League Baseball All-Star Game planned to be hosted on July 14 by the LA Dodgers is cancelled due to government restrictions imposed during the COVID-19 pandemic
MLB Cleveland Indians owner Paul Dolan announces he would review changing the organization's name
Mudslides crash through town of Atami, Japan, after torrential rain, killing 19, with over 100 people initially missing
Worst wildfires in Cyprus' history begin in Limassol district, spreading in next few days and killing four people
Sri Lanka says it has less than one day's worth of fuel, as its economic crisis deepens, closing schools and stopping public transport
Hottest global temperature ever recorded as average global temperature tops 17.01 degrees Celsius (previous record 16.92 degrees Celsius August 13, 2016)
Israel launches its biggest military operation against militants in the Jenin refugee camp, West Bank, in 20 years, killing 8 people and injuring 100 according to Palestinian authorities
State of Emergency declared in Yakutia region, Siberia, as more than 110 forest fires rage across 61,000 hectares (151,000 acres)
Earliest example of narrative cave art is dated to 51,200 years ago, from Indonesian caves of Maros-Pangkep, South Sulawesi, depicting people hunting a pig
Hurricane Beryl passes 45 miles south of Kingston, Jamaica, as a Category 4 hurricane with winds of 140 mph
Israel approves largest land grab in occupied West Bank in three decades, 12.7 square km (nearly 5 square m) in the Jordan Valley, aiming to prevent a Palestinian state
Researchers learn Hippos can fly - or at least become airborne - in a UK study of their footfall patterns showed them trotting at such speed their feet all left the ground
Archaeologists announce the discovery of a 3,500-year-old ancient city in Peru named Peñico
Russia becomes the first country to recognize the Taliban government in Afghanistan
Battle of Adrianople: Roman EmperorConstantine Idefeats his co-emperor Licinius, who flees to Byzantium
Treaty of Delft signed between Jacqueline of Bavaria andPhilip the Goodof Burgundy, ending the Hook and Cod wars in the County of Holland
Samuel de Champlainfounds the city of Quebec
George Washingtonsurrenders to French, Fort Necessity (7 Years' War)
Pioneering French magicianJean Eugène Robert-Houdinopens his magic theatre in Paris
In Germany,Karl Benzfirst drives the first automobile in Mannheim at a top speed of 16 km/h (10 mph)
KingLeopold IIgives Congo, previously a private possession, to Belgium
NY Giants pitcherRube MarquardtiesTim Keefe's 1888 MLB record 19 game win-streak with 2-1 win v Brooklyn Dodgers; has 21 with 2 end-of-season in 1911
Intergovernmental Conference on Identity Certificates for Russian Refugees, convened byFridtjof Nansenin Geneva, creates the Nansen passports for stateless persons
Harry Fergusongranted a patent for his three-point hitch for tractors
John Logie Bairddemonstrates the first color television transmission in London
German boxerMax Schmelingbeats American Young Stribling by TKO in 15 in Cleveland in his first heavyweight title defense; first major fight broadcast live on national radio
Del Mar Turf Club, with croonerBing Crosbyas president and actor Pat O'Brien as a club officer, opens for racing
PresidentFranklin Rooseveltdedicates the Eternal Light Peace Memorial and lights the eternal flame at Gettysburg Battlefield
Ernst Heinkel demonstrates 800 kph (500 mph) rocket plane toAdolf Hitler
"Double Indemnity" film noir directed byBilly Wilderand starring Fred MacMurray and Barbara Stanwyck is released in Baltimore, Maryland
James F. Byrnesbecomes 49th US Secretary of State, succeeding Edward Stettinius
"TheAndy WilliamsShow" premieres on ABC as a summer series (later moves to CBS, and then to NBC)

Newport Jazz Festival, Newport, Rhode Island opens, 78, 000 attend over 4 days; performers include: George Benson; Blood, Sweat, and Tears;Jeff Beck;James Brown; Bill Evans; Sun Ra; Mothers of Invention; Dave Brubeck;Miles Davis; Stephane Grapelli; and Sly & The Family Stone

Atlanta International Pop Festival opens, 200,000 attend over 3 days; performers include Allman Brothers; Grand Funk Railroad,Jimi HendrixExperience; Richie Havens; Cat Mother & the All-Night Newsboys;B.B. King; Mott the Hoople; and John Sebastian
Pitching in MLB-record 13th consecutive game for the LA Dodgers, Mike Marshall savesTommy John's 4-1 win over the Cincinnati Reds
Brian Wilsonperforms with the Beach Boys for the first time after his 12 year stage absence, at Anaheim Stadium in California
Wimbledon Men's Tennis: Sweden'sBjörn BorgbeatsIlie Năstaseof Romania 6-4, 6-2, 9-7 for the first of five straight Wimbledon titles
Triple Crown championSeattle Slew's 9-race win streak ends in the Swaps Stakes at Hollywood Park, finishes 4th
American Calvin Smith sets new world record of 9.93 for 100m in Colorado Springs, beatsJim Hines1968 mark by 0.02 seconds
"Back to the Future" directed by Robert Zemeckis and starringMichael J. FoxandChristopher Lloydis released
CBS announces a 21% stock buy-back to thwartTed Turner's takeover
Controversial NY Mets MLB starDarryl Strawberrythreatens teammates for criticising his play; he is now an ordained minister
TheFatih Sultan MehmetBridge in Istanbul, Turkey is completed, providing the second connection between the continents of Europe and Asia over the Bosphorus
Detroit Red Wings legendSteve Yzermanofficially retires from the NHL, finishing with 692 goals and 1,755 points
English singer-songwriterLily Allenreleases her debut single "Smile"
America's Cup: Swiss defender Alinghi beatsTeam New Zealandby 1 second to take the series 5-2 off Valencia, Spain
President of EgyptMohamed Morsiis deposed by the military in a coup d'état with Adly Mansour appointed interim president
French PresidentEmmanuel Macronspeech at Palace of Versailles proposes radical government changes, including reduction of MPs
Cardi Bis the first female rapper to achieve two #1 hits on the US Billboard chart with "I Like It" featuringBad Bunnyand J Balvin
Former Malaysian Prime MinisterNajib Razakis arrested on corruption charges, accused of stealing 700 million from state development fund 1MDB
Trump's Justice and Education Departments rescindObamaera affirmative action policies for college admissions
RapperLL Cool Jwithdraws from 4th of July concert in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania in support of striking blue-collar municipal workers
Louis IXof France is captured by Baibars' Mamluk army at the Battle of Fariskur while he is in Egypt conducting the Seventh Crusade; he later has to ransom himself
Pony Express arrives in San Francisco with overland letters from NY
Battle of Gettysburg, the largest battle ever fought on the American continent, ends in a major victory for the Union during the US Civil War
British Royal Navy damages the French fleet in Mers-el-Kebir, Algeria, to prevent Germany seizing it
USPresident Reaganpresides over relighting of renovated Statue of Liberty
Go-Tsuchimikado, 103rd Emperor of Japan (1464-1500), born in Japan (d. 1500)
Li Shi-zhen, Chinese physician and medical scholar (Compendium of Materia Medica), born in Qizhou, China (d. 1593)
Claude Fauchet, French historian, born in Paris (d. 1601)
Jakob Handl [Petelin], German-Austrian composer (Opus musicum), and Cistercian monk, born in Reifnitz, Carniola (now Ribnica, Slovenia) (d. 1591)
Alessandro Stradella, Italian violinist and composer, born in Bologna, Papal States (d. 1682)
Leopold I, Prince of Anhalt-Dessau, Prussian field marshal, born in Dessau, Anhalt-Dessau (d. 1747)
Edward Young, English poet (Revenge, Complaint), born in Upham, Winchester, England (d. 1765)
Sir Robert Rich, 4th Baronet, British cavalry officer, born in Roos Hall, Beccles, England (d. 1768)
Arnold Hoogvliet, Dutch poet (Abraham the Patriarch), born in Vlaardingen, Netherlands (d. 1763)
Robert Adam, Scottish architect and designer (Adelphi Terrace, London), born in Kirkcaldy, Scotland (d. 1792)
Samuel Huntington, American politician and signer of the Declaration of Independence, born in Windham, Connecticut (d. 1796)
John Singleton Copley, American painter of portraits and historical objects, born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1815)
Sophia Magdalena of Denmark, Queen consort of Sweden, born in Christiansborg Palace, Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 1813)
Eberhard Friedrich Walcker, German organ builder (Paulskirche Frankfurt), born in Bad Cannstatt, Stuttgart, Germany (d. 1872)
Nikolai Poveloy, Russian writer and publisher (Sotsjinenija), born in Irkutsk, Russia (d. 1846) [NS]
Joseph Labitzky, Bohemian composer, born in Krásno, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1881)
Janis Cimze, Latvian composer, Father of Latvian choir music, born in Rauna parish, Russian Empire (d. 1881)
Louis Théodore Gouvy, German-French composer, born in Sarre, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1898)
John Austin Wharton, American lawyer and Major General (Confederate Army), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1865)
Achilles Alferaki, Russian composer, born in Kharkov, Russian Empire (d. 1919)
Alfredo Keil, Portuguese composer, born in Lisbon, Kingdom of Portugal (d. 1907)
Piotr Maszyński, Polish composer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1934)
Charlotte Perkins Gilman, American feminist, writer and lecturer for social reform, born in Hartford, Connecticut (d. 1935)
William Wallace, Scottish composer, born in Greenock, Scotland (d. 1940)
Peter Jackson, Australian heavyweight boxer (International Boxing Hall of Fame), born in Christiansted, US Virgin Islands (d. 1901)
Friedrich Ernst Koch, German composer, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1927)
Albert Gottschalk, Danish painter, born in Stege, Møn, Denmark (d. 1906)
Vicente Arregui Garay, Spanish composer, born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1925)
William Henry Davies, Welsh poet (Autobiography of a Super Tramp), born in Newport, Monmouthshire, Wales (d. 1940)
Habibullah Khan, Emir of Afghanistan (1901-19), born in Samarkand, Uzbekistan (d. 1919)
Āpirana Ngata, Māori New Zealand politician and lawyer known for promoting and protecting Māori culture and language, born in Te Araroa, New Zealand (d. 1950)
Ernst Ferdinand Sauerbruch, German Nazi surgeon, born in Barmen, German Empire (d. 1951)
Alfred Korzybski, Polish-American scientist and philosopher, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1950)
Philippe Gaubert, French flautist, conductor (Paris Opéra, 1919-41), composer, and teacher (Paris Conservatoire), born in Cahors, France (d. 1941)
Carl Schuricht, German conductor and composer, born in Gdańsk, Poland (d. 1967)
Leon Errol, Australian actor (Ziegfeld Follies, What a Blonde!), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1951)
Moses Leverock Crossley, Dutch bio-chemist, born in Saba, Dutch West Indies (d. 1971)
Raymond A. Spruance, U.S. Navy admiral (Battle of Midway), born in Baltimore, Maryland (d. 1969)
Wilhelm Rettich, German composer, born in Leipzig, German Empire (d. 1988)
Oles' Semyonovich Chishko, Russian-Ukrainian composer, born in Dvorichnyi Kut, Ukraine (d. 1976)
Doris Lloyd, Irish-American actress (Bachelor Father, Charley's Aunt, Sarah & Son), born in Liverpool, England (d. 1968)
Klimenty Arkad'yevich Korchmaryov, Ukrainian composer, born in Verkhnodniprovsk, Ukraine (d. 1958)
Ludwig Guttmann, German-British neurologist, founder of the Stoke Mandeville Games (now the Paralympics), born in Tost, Prussia, German Empire (now Toszek, Poland) (d. 1980)
Otto Reinhold, German composer, and music educator, born in Thum, Saxony, Germany (d. 1965)
John Mason Brown, American drama critic (Tonight on Broadway), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1969)
Ruth Crawford Seeger, American modernist classical and folk music composer (String Quartet, 1931; Rissolty, Rossolty), born in East Liverpool, Ohio (d. 1953)
Jack Newman, New Zealand cricket fast bowler (3 Tests; Test selector 1958-63; president NZ Cricket Council 1964-67), born in Brightwater, NZ (d. 1996)
Ralph Samuelson, American outdoorsman considered the inventor an water-skiing, and later turkey farmer, born in Lake City, Minnesota (d. 1977)[1]
F. Gwendolen Rees, Welsh zoologist and parasitologist, born in Abercynon, Wales (d. 1994)
Francis Steegmuller, American biographer (Cocteau), born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1994)
George Sanders, Russian actor (All About Eve-Academy Award 1950), born in St. Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1972)
Jack Earle, American actor and sideshow performer known as "The World's Tallest Man", born in Denver, Colorado (d. 1952)
Arthur Staal, Dutch architect (Group '32), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Gene Gutchë [Romeo Eugene Gutschë], German-American composer (Akhenaten; Bongo Divertimento), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2000)
M. F. K. Fisher, American cook book author, born in Albion, Michigan (d. 1992)
Robert B. Meyner, American politician (44th Governor of New Jersey), born in Easton, Pennsylvania (d. 1990)
Thomas Narcejac, French writer (Vertigo), born in Rochefort-sur-Mer, France (d. 1998)
Earl Butz, 18th U.S. Secretary of Agriculture (1971-76), born in Albion, Indiana (d. 2008)
Lucy Kroll, American talent agent, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1997)
Stavros Niarchos, Greek shipping magnate (Those Fabulous Greeks), born in Athens, Greece (d. 1996)
Joe Hardstaff Jr., English cricket batsman (23 Tests, 1,636 runs @ 46.74; Nottinghamshire CCC), born in Nuncargate, Nottinghamshire (d. 1990)
Hugh Stirling Mackenzie, British Royal Navy officer, born in Inverness, Scotland (d. 1996)
William Deakin, British historian and warden (St Anthony's College Oxford), born in London (d. 2005)
Jerry Gray [Generoso Graziano], American violinist, arranger (Artie Shaw, Glenn Miller). and composer("Pennsylvania 6-5000", "A String of Pearls"), born in East Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1976)
Hélène Cordet, French actress (The Limping Man), born in Marseille, Bouches-du-Rhône, France (d. 1996)
João Saldanha, Brazilian journalist and football manager (Brazil 1969-70), born in Alegrete, Brazil (d. 1990)
Fred Mulley, British Labour politician, born in Leamington Spa, England (d. 1995)
John Lessard, American neo-classical composer, born in San Francisco, California (d. 2003)
Louise Allbritton, American actress (The Egg and I, Stage Door, Got a Secret), born in Oklahoma City (d. 1979)
Susan Peters [Suzanne Carnahan], American actress (Random Harvest, Young Ideas), born in Spokane, Washington (d. 1952)
Corneille [Cornelis G of Beverloo], Dutch painter (Africa, Antilles), born in Liege, Belgium (d. 2010)
François Reichenbach, French director (La douceur du Village), born in Paris (d. 1993)
Tom Hudson, British artist and teacher, born in Horden, County Durham, England (d. 1997)
Bankole Timothy, Sierra Leonean journalist, born in Sierra Leone (d. 1994)
Johnny Hartman, American jazz ballad singer (John Coltrane and Johnny Hartman - "My One And Only Love"), born in Houma, Louisiana (d. 1983)
Sue Ryder, Baroness Ryder of Warsaw & Cavendish, British volunteer with Special Operations Executive in the Second World War, born in Leeds, Yorkshire, England (d. 2000)
William Mills, English painter, born in Hampshire (d. 1997)
S. R. [Sellapan Ramanathan] Nathan, 6th President of Singapore (1999-2011), born in Singapore (d. 2016)
Michael Oliver, British cardiologist (linked cholesterol with coronary artery disease), born in Borth, Wales (d. 2015)
Johnny Coles, American jazz trumpeter (Little Johnny C), born in Trenton, New Jersey (d. 1997)
Joseph M. Gaydos, American politician (Member of the U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania), born in Braddock, Pennsylvania (d. 2015)
Meyer Kupferman, American composer (Infinities), clarinetist, and educator (Sarah Lawrence College, 1951-94), born in New York City (d. 2003)[1]
Rae Allen [Raffaellla Abruzzo], American stage and screen actress, born on Brooklyn, New York City (d. 2022)
William Roll, German poltergeist researcher and parapsychologist (The Poltergeist, Unsolved Mysteries), born in Bremen, Germany (d. 2012)
Charles Vandenhove, Belgian architect (Standard-Omnisporthal, Liège), born in Teuven, Belgium (d. 2019)
Ken Russell, British film director (Tommy, Altered States, Gothic), born in Southampton, Hampshire, England (d. 2011)
William MacMillan, Moderator of the General Assembly of Church of Scotland, born in Keith, Moray, Scotland (d. 2002)
Evelyn Anthony, English historical writer (Poellenberg Inheritance), born in London (d. 2018)
Günter Bruno Fuchs, German writer, poet and artist, born in Berlin (d. 1977)
John Wills, Lord-Lieutenant of Avon 1974-96, born in Bristol (d. 1998)
David Lynch, American singer (Platters - "Twilight Time"; "Only You"; "The Great Pretender"), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1980)
Billy Howton, American football end (First-team All-Pro 1956, 57; Pro Bowl 1952, 1955–57 Green Bay Packers), born in Littlefield, Texas (d. 2025)
Pete Fountain, American jazz clarinetist (Lawrence Welk, 1957-59), born in New Orleans, Louisiana (d. 2016)
Tommy Tedesco, American jazz and Los Angeles session guitarist (Wrecking Crew; Lalo Schifrin), born in Niagra Falls, New York (d. 1997)
[Karl] Carlos Kleiber, Austrian-Argentine conductor (Bavarian State Orchestra, 1968-97), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2004)
Andres Burnier [Catharina Irma Dessaur], Dutch criminologist and author (Jongensuur), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 2002)
Frits Helmuth, Danish actor My Son Peter; Per), born in Copenhagen, Denmark (d. 2004)
Robert O. Ragland, American film score composer (The Thing With Two Heads), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
Edward Brandt Jr., American physician and public health official, born in Oklahoma (d. 2007)
Manfred Bieler, German writer (The Rabbit is Me), born in Zerbst, Saxony-Anhalt, Germany (d. 2002)
Roger Christian, American radio personality and lyricist ("Don’t Worry Baby"; "Little Deuce Coupe"; "The Little Old Lady From Pasadena"), born in Buffalo, New York (d. 1991)
Stefan Abadzhiev, Bulgarian soccer forward (27 caps; PFC Levski Sofia), born in Sofia, Bulgaria (d. 2024)
(José ) "Cheo" Feliciano, Puerto Rican salsa and bolero singer and composer (Joe Cuba Sextet; Eddie Palmieri Orchestra), and anti0drug advocate, born in Ponce, Puerto Rico (d. 2014)
Harrison Schmitt, American geologist and astronaut (Apollo 17), born in Santa Rita, New Mexico
John Swan, 4th Premier of Bermuda (1982-95), born in Bermuda
Anthony Lester, British barrister, Member of the House of Lords who influenced race relations legislation, born in London (d. 2020)
Eric Russell, English cricket batsman (10 Tests, 2 x 50, HS 70; Middlesex CCC, Berkshire CCC), born in Dumbarton, Scotland
Leo Wilden, German soccer defender (15 caps West Germany; 1. FC Köln, Bayer Leverkusen), born in Düren, Germany (d. 2022)
